metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the coaching note schema extension: specify the new field name (e.g. `guide_url`), whether it is added to the shared kernel `render_coach_notes` or only to a plan-review-specific post-processing step, and confirm that `code_review/workflow_ops.py` is explicitly out of scope for this change.
findings.finding: The plan directs `render_coach_notes` (in `passes.py` and the shared `review_kernel/coach.py`) to emit URL#anchors, but the current coaching note output schema is `{move_id, move_name, subject, finding_refs, coaching}` — no `url` or `guide_link` field exists. The plan never specifies: (a) which field carries the deep-link, (b) whether it goes in the shared kernel's `render_coach_notes` (which is also used by `code_review/workflow_ops.py`, creating a cross-domain coupling) or only in the plan-review-specific path, (c) how the locked template mechanism interacts with a dynamically-injected URL. The coaching note schema extension is entirely unspecified.
findings.location: What section — 'Coach anchored deep-links: Pass-4 renderer (passes.py) + CLI text renderer emit URL#anchor'; AC5 — 'Coach output emits stable anchored deep-links'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the anchor casing rule: canonicalize to `#<criterion-id-lowercased>` (or explicit case-preserved) in BOTH the What section and AC4 so the renderer, guide headings, and parity check all share one rule.
findings.finding: The anchor rule is specified two different ways: the What section says '#<criterion-id lower-cased>' (explicitly lowercase) while AC4 says '#<criterion-id>' with no casing instruction, creating ambiguity for both the renderer implementer and the parity checker.
findings.location: What section ('ANCHOR RULE: `#<criterion-id lower-cased>`') vs AC4 ('anchor rule `#<criterion-id>`')
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC4 to read: 'anchor rule `#<criterion-id-lowercased>`' and add a note in the What section that guide headings MUST use lowercase criterion ids.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the anchor casing rule between the What section and AC4 — pick one canonical form (recommend lowercase to match standard Markdown slug behavior) and use it consistently in both places.
findings.finding: The anchor rule is specified two incompatible ways. The What section says `#<criterion-id lower-cased>` (explicitly lowercase), while AC4 says `#<criterion-id>` with no casing instruction. The parity check (which must validate heading slugs vs emitted anchors) cannot be correctly implemented without a single canonical rule. This is a co-located protocol contradiction between the spec and its own AC.
findings.location: What section — 'ANCHOR RULE: `#<criterion-id lower-cased>`'; AC4 — 'anchor rule `#<criterion-id>`'
findings.suggested_fix: Standardize on `#<criterion-id lower-cased>` (the What section form) and update AC4 to read 'anchor rule `#<criterion-id-lowercased>`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the `REBAR_DOCS_URL` default constant to a narrower home (e.g., `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` module or `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py`) rather than `src/rebar/config.py`, unless there is an affirmative reason it must be in the core config layer (e.g., it needs layered TOML/env resolution — in which case that should be stated explicitly).
findings.finding: The plan places `REBAR_DOCS_URL` as a config constant in `src/rebar/config.py`, but `config.py` is the core rebar config module (imported tree-wide, covering tracker/sync/jira/mcp/verify config). A docs-base-URL for plan-review coaching output is a narrow, plan-review–specific concern that does not need layered config merging or TOML persistence. Adding it there either (a) makes it a full typed config key — config-surface proliferation — or (b) makes it a bare string constant that has no business in the core config module.
findings.location: What section: 'add the default as a config constant in src/rebar/config.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a module-level constant `DOCS_BASE_URL_DEFAULT` in e.g. `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py` or a small `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/_docs.py` helper. Read `os.environ.get('REBAR_DOCS_URL')` at call sites. This keeps the concern local without a config-schema entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a parity check (e.g., in the validate-routing step or a dedicated test) that compares the expected anchor `#<criterion-id lower-cased>` against the actual heading slugs present in the guide file, and document the heading-slug contract in config.py or the guide's header so authors know the constraint.
findings.finding: The anchor rule `#<criterion-id lower-cased>` is a coupling contract between the guide's per-criterion heading slugs and every link emitter (passes.py, _cli/_llm_commands.py), but the plan neither captures it as a documented contract that guide authors must honour nor adds a parity check that validates emitted anchors against actual heading anchors in the guide file. A heading rename or a Markdown renderer that slugifies differently (e.g., spaces→hyphens, special chars stripped) silently produces 404 deep-links with no CI signal.
findings.location: What / Coach anchored deep-links; AC line 'anchor rule #<criterion-id>'
findings.suggested_fix: In the validate_packaged_routing (or new check), parse the guide Markdown for all H2/H3 headings, slugify them with the same rule the plan specifies (`lower-cased`, spaces→hyphens), and assert each criterion id appears as a slug. Add a one-line contract comment in config.py near the ANCHOR_RULE constant: `# Guide headings must be exactly '## <CRITERION_ID>' (case-insensitive) to satisfy this rule.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that deep-link URLs are added as a separate structured field (e.g. `guide_url`) on each coaching note dict — NOT embedded in the existing `coaching` prose string — so CLI, MCP, and sidecar consumers can each render or ignore the link as appropriate, and the locked-template contract remains intact.
findings.finding: The mechanism for injecting deep-link URLs into coach output is unspecified at the data-structure level, creating an ambiguity that makes the MCP consumer incompatible with the proposed approach. `render_coach_notes` (in `review_kernel/coach.py`) builds coaching prose as `move['template'].format(subject=subject)` and packages the result as `{'move_id':…, 'coaching': <prose string>}`. The plan says 'Pass-4 renderer (passes.py) + CLI text renderer emit URL#anchor' but doesn't specify whether (a) the URL is appended to the prose string inside the existing `coaching` field, or (b) a separate `guide_url` field is added to each coaching note dict. Approach (a) breaks the locked-template contract and conflates navigation metadata into prose. Approach (b) — a separate structured field — is codebase-aligned (the verdict dict is already a structured payload) and lets each consumer (CLI, MCP, sidecar) render the URL as it chooses. Without specifying (b), `code_review/workflow_ops.py` and the MCP tool may receive URL strings embedded in prose rather than navigable links, especially on `file://` paths in MCP contexts where the client is on a different machine.
findings.location: What section — 'Coach anchored deep-links: Pass-4 renderer (passes.py) + CLI text renderer (_cli/_llm_commands.py _render_plan_review_text) emit URL#anchor'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's 'Pass-4 renderer (passes.py)' attribution: render_coach_notes is a thin re-export in passes.py; the actual implementation to extend with URL#anchor emission is review_kernel/coach.py (or the workflow_ops.py caller that assembles coaching[]).
findings.finding: The plan says 'Pass-4 renderer (passes.py) … emit URL#anchor', directing the implementer to modify passes.py. But passes.py:74 confirms render_coach_notes is only a THIN RE-EXPORT (`render_coach_notes = _coach.render_coach_notes`) from review_kernel/coach.py, which contains the real implementation (lines 162-185). Modifying passes.py cannot add URL#anchor emission — only modifying review_kernel/coach.py (or its callers) can.
findings.location: What section — 'Coach anchored deep-links: Pass-4 renderer (passes.py) … emit URL#anchor'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Pass-4 renderer (passes.py)' with 'Pass-4 renderer (review_kernel/coach.py — render_coach_notes; re-exported via passes.py)' and note that the URL injection point is inside review_kernel/coach.py:render_coach_notes or in the workflow_ops.py step that calls it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'native-intercept in _cli/_help.py known_subcommands' claim: known_subcommands() discovers help text from .txt files only; the actual native-intercept pattern for rebar explain is an explicit `if argv[0] == 'explain':` block in __init__.py, plus an optional explain.txt in _cli/help/ if help-text discovery is desired.
findings.finding: The plan says 'native-intercept in _cli/_help.py known_subcommands' as the registration point for the `rebar explain` subcommand. But known_subcommands() is driven purely by the presence of .txt files under src/rebar/_cli/help/ (confirmed _help.py:60-66: it iterates importlib.resources files ending in .txt). Native intercepts such as review-plan, criteria, prompt, and verify-completion are registered via explicit `if argv[0] == 'X':` blocks in __init__.py, not via known_subcommands. Listing 'known_subcommands' as the registration mechanism for a native intercept is a false characterization of how that function works.
findings.location: What section — 'rebar explain <criterion-id>: … CLI dispatch arm in src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py + native-intercept in _cli/_help.py known_subcommands'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'native-intercept in _cli/_help.py known_subcommands' with: 'add an explicit `if argv and argv[0] == "explain":` dispatch arm in __init__.py (following the review-plan/criteria pattern) and optionally add src/rebar/_cli/help/explain.txt for help-text discovery via known_subcommands'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py` to the edit-target list for the URL#anchor emission change (it is the real home of `render_coach_notes`; passes.py only re-exports it).
findings.finding: The plan attributes URL#anchor emission to `passes.py` (Pass-4 renderer) and `_cli/_llm_commands.py _render_plan_review_text`, but the actual `render_coach_notes` implementation lives in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py` — `passes.py` only re-exports it. Modifying the coach note structure (adding a `url` field) requires editing `review_kernel/coach.py`, which is not listed as an edit target.
findings.location: What section: 'Pass-4 renderer (passes.py) + CLI text renderer (_cli/_llm_commands.py _render_plan_review_text) emit URL#anchor'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `rebar explain` CLI wiring to match the codebase's native-intercept pattern: add an `if argv[0] == 'explain': return _explain(argv[1:])` block in `_cli/__init__.py main()` BEFORE the `known_subcommands()` check — no help `.txt` file and no `_dispatch()` arm needed (consistent with review-plan, criteria, prompt, etc.).
findings.finding: The plan's CLI wiring instruction — 'native-intercept in `_cli/__init__.py` + native-intercept in `_cli/_help.py` known_subcommands' — conflates two mutually-incompatible dispatch mechanisms. In the actual codebase, native intercepts (review, review-plan, criteria, etc.) are added as `if argv[0] == 'explain': return _explain(argv[1:])` BEFORE the `known_subcommands()` gate in `main()`. `known_subcommands()` is driven by the presence of `.txt` files in the `_cli/help/` package-data directory and is checked only AFTER native intercepts fire. Adding `explain` to `known_subcommands()` alone would require both a help `.txt` file AND a `_dispatch()` arm — but then the intercept in `main()` would already have consumed it, leaving the `_dispatch` arm as dead code. As written the spec is mechanically incorrect for at least one of the two registration sites.
findings.location: What section — `rebar explain <criterion-id>`: CLI dispatch arm in src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py + native-intercept in _cli/_help.py known_subcommands
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'CLI dispatch arm in src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py + native-intercept in _cli/_help.py known_subcommands' with: 'add an `if argv[0] == "explain":` early-intercept in `_cli/__init__.py main()`, before the `known_subcommands()` gate; no help/.txt file needed (no golden capture — matches review-plan, criteria, prompt precedent). _help.known_subcommands need NOT be updated.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the `explain_criterion` MCP tool is registered via `register_read_tools` in `_mcp_reads.py` (not `register_llm_tools` in `_mcp_llm.py`), consistent with the existing architectural split: `_mcp_llm` exclusively hosts `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`-gated LLM-call tools.
findings.finding: The plan registers the `explain_criterion` MCP tool via `register_llm_tools` in `_mcp_llm.py`, but the existing architecture of that module exclusively gates every registered tool on `_allow_llm()` (requires `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM=1`). An explain lookup makes no LLM call and should instead be registered via `register_read_tools` in `_mcp_reads.py` — the established seam for non-LLM read operations. Placing it in `register_llm_tools` would either gate a pure-read tool behind the LLM flag or require a guard bypass that breaks the module's invariant.
findings.location: What section: 'rebar explain <criterion-id>... an MCP tool in src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py via register_llm_tools'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, change 'an MCP tool in src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py via register_llm_tools' to 'an MCP tool in src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py via register_read_tools (no LLM call; belongs in the read-tool cluster)'. Update AC3 proof accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the two-part wiring for `rebar explain`: (a) add an early `if argv[0] == "explain"` intercept arm in `_cli/__init__.py` main() (matching the pattern used by review, review-plan, criteria), AND (b) create `src/rebar/_cli/help/explain.txt` so `known_subcommands()` includes it and `rebar help explain` / `rebar explain --help` work — the plan's reference to `_help.py known_subcommands` alone is not sufficient.
findings.finding: The plan says 'native-intercept in _cli/_help.py known_subcommands' for the 'rebar explain' subcommand, but known_subcommands() in _help.py is derived ONLY from .txt package-data files in rebar/_cli/help/ — it is NOT an editable registry. All existing native-intercept subcommands (review, review-plan, criteria, prompt, llm, etc.) bypass known_subcommands entirely via early if-arm interception in main() before line 450. Naming known_subcommands as the wiring point conflates two separate mechanisms: (a) early-intercept routing (the correct pattern, requires an if-arm in main()) and (b) help-text registration (requires a .txt file so 'rebar help explain' and 'rebar explain --help' work). The plan must specify both steps or the subcommand either silently falls through to the 'unknown subcommand' error or bypasses the help system.
findings.location: What section: 'rebar explain <criterion-id>: ONE shared library lookup... wrapped by (a) a CLI dispatch arm in src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py + native-intercept in _cli/_help.py known_subcommands'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's What section, replace 'native-intercept in _cli/_help.py known_subcommands' with: '(i) an early if-arm in main() (same pattern as review-plan/criteria) and (ii) a new src/rebar/_cli/help/explain.txt help file so known_subcommands() includes it and the help system works'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `rebar explain` CLI wiring: specify (a) add an `if argv and argv[0] == 'explain':` intercept in `_cli/__init__.py` (the native-intercept pattern), AND (b) ship an `explain.txt` help file under `rebar._cli.help/` so `known_subcommands()` picks it up — remove the erroneous claim that `known_subcommands` itself needs code changes.
findings.finding: `known_subcommands()` in `_cli/_help.py` is derived PURELY from `.txt` package-data files under `rebar._cli.help/` — it has no programmatic registration mechanism. The plan instructs adding `explain` as a 'native-intercept in `_cli/__init__.py` + native-intercept in `_cli/_help.py` known_subcommands', but every other native intercept (`review`, `review-plan`, `criteria`, `prompt`, etc.) is handled by an `if argv and argv[0] == "<sub>":` guard BEFORE the `known_subcommands()` gate at line 450, NOT by registration in `known_subcommands`. The plan's wiring protocol conflates two incompatible dispatch paths.
findings.location: What section — 'rebar explain' CLI bullet; `_cli/__init__.py` dispatch; `_cli/_help.py` known_subcommands

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `explain` is registered in `_mcp_llm.py` (with LLM-gate bypass reasoning) or in the existing `_mcp_reads.py` module for read-only tools, and state explicitly that the LLM gate does NOT apply to this tool.
findings.finding: The plan routes `explain_criterion` as an MCP tool through `register_llm_tools` in `_mcp_llm.py`, but every tool in that module is gated on `_allow_llm()` (which guards billable LLM calls). `explain_criterion` is a pure registry/file read with no LLM call. The plan never specifies whether the `explain` tool bypasses the LLM gate, uses a separate registration path, or is placed in a different module (`_mcp_reads.py` already exists for read-only tools). The fallback protocol when `_allow_llm` is False is unspecified for this tool.
findings.location: What section — 'an MCP tool in src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py via register_llm_tools'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Register the `explain` MCP tool in `_mcp_reads.py` (not `_mcp_llm.py`) — it is a pure registry read, not a billable LLM call, so the `_allow_llm` gate does not apply.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all `coaching[]` consumers that `render_coach_notes` output flows through (`plan_review/workflow_ops.py`, `plan_review/sidecar.py`, `plan_review/orchestrator.py`, `code_review/sidecar.py`, `workflow/gate_dispatch.py`, `code_review/shim.py`) and confirm each is passthrough-safe with a new `url` field or explicitly listed as a no-update target.
findings.finding: The plan names only `code_review/workflow_ops.py` as a downstream `coaching[]` consumer to update, but the coaching output is consumed by at least five additional modules outside `code_review/`: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py` (line 425), `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` (line 513), `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` (line 286), `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` (line 34), and `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` (multiple lines). If `render_coach_notes` gains a new `url` field on each note dict, all of these consumers either need to handle it or remain passthrough-safe.
findings.location: What section: 'Downstream consumer to update: code_review/workflow_ops.py consumes render_coach_notes / coaching[] output' and AC: 'code_review/workflow_ops.py still consumes coaching[] without breakage'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the deep-link URL#anchor is added as a POST-PROCESSING step in `plan_review/workflow_ops.py` (after calling `render_coach_notes`) or in `_render_plan_review_text`, NOT by modifying the shared `review_kernel.render_coach_notes` — to avoid forcing the code-review path to carry plan-review guide URLs.
findings.finding: The plan requires the Pass-4 coach deep-link to be emitted by `passes.py`'s renderer, but `render_coach_notes` in `passes.py` is a module-level re-export alias of `rebar.llm.review_kernel.coach.render_coach_notes` — the SHARED kernel function also used by `code_review/workflow_ops.py`. If the URL#anchor is baked into `render_coach_notes`, every code-review coaching call also emits plan-review–specific guide links, which is a golden-hammer coupling of a plan-review authoring-guide URL into the code-review coaching path.
findings.location: What section: 'Coach anchored deep-links: Pass-4 renderer (passes.py) + CLI text renderer (_cli/_llm_commands.py _render_plan_review_text) emit URL#anchor'
findings.suggested_fix: In `plan_review/workflow_ops.py plan_review_coach()`, after `coaching = passes.render_coach_notes(...)`, add a post-processing pass that appends `url` to each note dict. In `_render_plan_review_text`, emit the URL from the note's `url` field. The kernel function stays unchanged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description of the `review-plan --help` epilog deliverable to the What section (or the R-5 bullet), including the expected format and fallback behavior when REBAR_DOCS_URL is unset.
findings.finding: AC4 requires 'review-plan --help epilog carries the base URL' but this deliverable is not described anywhere in the What section body, making it an orphan acceptance criterion with no backing specification.
findings.location: AC4 vs What section ('Coach anchored deep-links' bullet)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that the _review_plan() ArgumentParser in _cli/_llm_commands.py must be updated with epilog=<base_url_constant> so the AC ('review-plan --help epilog carries the base URL') is wirable — the current code has no epilog= parameter.
findings.finding: The plan's AC requires 'review-plan --help epilog carries the base URL', but the existing _review_plan() in _cli/_llm_commands.py constructs its ArgumentParser without an epilog= parameter, and the plan gives no instruction to add one. Native-intercept subcommands own their own argparse instances (not the pinned .txt help system), so adding an epilog requires an explicit epilog= kwarg in the ArgumentParser constructor call — a code change the plan does not name.
findings.location: AC line: 'review-plan --help epilog carries the base URL'; What section: 'BASE URL: defaults to the REBAR_DOCS_URL env var if set...'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Update the ArgumentParser in _review_plan() (_cli/_llm_commands.py) to pass epilog=f"Criterion guide: {REBAR_DOCS_BASE_URL}" (resolved at parse-time or via a lazy default).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit test coverage for both URL-source branches in `pytest -k coach_deeplink` (or two named variants): one asserting the emitted URL uses REBAR_DOCS_URL when set, and one asserting the file:// fallback when the env var is absent.
findings.finding: The deep-link base-URL logic has two distinct branches (REBAR_DOCS_URL env var set vs. unset → relative file:// path), but the only named test is `pytest -k coach_deeplink`, which is described as asserting 'a URL#anchor is emitted' — singular, with no explicit coverage of both URL-source branches. The env-var override path (or the fallback path) could regress silently.
findings.location: AC: 'Base URL default (REBAR_DOCS_URL env else relative file://) as a config.py constant' + 'pytest -k coach_deeplink'
findings.suggested_fix: Parameterize the coach_deeplink test with `@pytest.mark.parametrize('env,expected_prefix', [('https://example.com/docs', 'https://example.com/docs'), (None, 'file://')])` and assert the emitted URL starts with the expected prefix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a happy-path acceptance criterion or unit test for the MCP `explain` tool: assert that `explain_criterion('F1')` via the MCP tool returns the expected criterion content (not just that errors are structured).
findings.finding: The MCP tool surface for `rebar explain` has explicit error-path AC ('MCP tool returns a structured error') but no stated happy-path acceptance criterion — there is no 'MCP explain F1 returns the criterion content' assertion. The MCP binding could be wired incorrectly (e.g., always returning an error payload, or returning an empty result) and the test suite would still pass green.
findings.location: AC: 'rebar explain <criterion-id> in CLI + MCP + library off ONE shared lookup. Proof: rebar explain F1 prints the section ... AND the MCP tool returns a structured error'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a sub-bullet to AC2) verifying that the auto-generated vs hand-maintained decision is recorded (e.g., in an ADR, a comment in registry.py, or a note in the guide itself).
findings.finding: The What section requires a recorded design decision ('Decide auto-generated vs hand-maintained-with-parity (recommend generated) and record it') but no AC verifies that the decision is documented, leaving this deliverable uncovered.
findings.location: What section, R-5 bullet ('Decide auto-generated vs hand-maintained-with-parity (recommend generated) and record it') vs Acceptance Criteria section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name a specific location for the auto-generated vs hand-maintained decision record (e.g., a dedicated section in docs/plan-review-gate.md, a docs/adr/ file, or a header comment in the generated guide file) so the AC is verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan directs the implementer to 'decide auto-generated vs hand-maintained-with-parity (recommend generated) and record it,' but does not specify WHERE this decision is recorded — no ADR file, no doc section, no README note is named. The decision record will be unlocatable or silently omitted.
findings.location: What section — 'R-5 (SEQUENCE AFTER WS1-8)' paragraph, sentence: 'Decide auto-generated vs hand-maintained-with-parity (recommend generated) and record it.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Record the decision in a `## Guide generation` section at the top of the authoring guide file itself (or in docs/plan-review-gate.md), including the rationale.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/explain.txt` to the explicit edit-target list — this package-data file is required for `known_subcommands()` to recognize `explain` as a valid subcommand.
findings.finding: Adding `rebar explain` to the CLI requires creating a new package-data file `src/rebar/_cli/help/explain.txt`. The `known_subcommands()` function in `_cli/_help.py` discovers commands by listing `*.txt` files in the help package — it is NOT a hand-maintained list. Without `explain.txt`, `rebar explain` is routed to the unknown-subcommand path even if the intercept arm is added to `_cli/__init__.py`. This edit target is absent from the plan.
findings.location: What section: 'a CLI dispatch arm in src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py + native-intercept in _cli/_help.py known_subcommands'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/explain.txt` (the usage string for `rebar explain <criterion-id>`) to the What section's edit-target list alongside the `__init__.py` dispatch arm.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the MCP error payload schema for `explain` — e.g. `{"error": "unknown-id"|"malformed-registry"|"missing-file", "criterion_id": str, "message": str}` — and add it to the What section or the AC3 proof specification.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the MCP `explain` tool 'returns a structured error payload' for unknown-id, malformed-registry, and missing-file, but never defines what that payload looks like. Every existing tool in `_mcp_llm.py` raises `ValueError` for error states (no structured dict). The plan introduces a NEW error-state protocol ('structured error') without specifying the output schema fields.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP tool in _mcp_llm.py'; AC3 ('MCP tool returns a structured error')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify explicitly whether workflow_ops.py needs code changes: state either 'no changes required — coaching[] entries are passed through as opaque dicts' or 'add field access for the new `deep_link` key at line X'.
findings.finding: The role of code_review/workflow_ops.py is described ambiguously: the plan says it 'consumes render_coach_notes / coaching[] output' and the AC says it must consume the new output 'without breakage', but it is never stated whether the file requires ACTIVE modification to handle the new URL#anchor field or whether it will transparently pass through an opaque string. An executor cannot know whether to open and edit that file.
findings.location: What section: 'Downstream consumer to update: code_review/workflow_ops.py consumes render_coach_notes / coaching[] output' and AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command to the R-3 AC, e.g. `grep -c 'fails-open\|fails-closed\|errs-toward' docs/plan-review-gate.md | xargs -I{} test {} -ge 5` or a dedicated `pytest -k plan_review_gate_doc` that asserts the required section headings exist.
findings.finding: AC for R-3 ('R-3 invariant + tuning checklist in docs/plan-review-gate.md') has no machine-verifiable proving command — there is no grep, file-check, or CI step named to confirm the invariant prose and checklist are present with required content.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: first bullet '[ ] R-3 invariant + tuning checklist in docs/plan-review-gate.md'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC5 into two criteria: (1) 'Coach output emits URL#anchor; Proof: pytest -k coach_deeplink asserts anchor format' and (2) 'workflow_ops.py consumes updated coaching[] without error; Proof: pytest -k workflow_ops_coach_compat (or add an assertion to the existing coach_deeplink test that exercises the workflow_ops parse path directly)'.
findings.finding: AC5 ('Coach output emits stable anchored deep-links; code_review/workflow_ops.py still consumes coaching[] without breakage') bundles two distinct, independently-falsifiable outcomes — deep-link emission and downstream consumer compatibility — into a single criterion, and 'without breakage' has no associated proving command beyond the pytest -k coach_deeplink test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: fifth bullet '[ ] Coach output emits stable anchored deep-links; code_review/workflow_ops.py still consumes coaching[] without breakage'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or document) that `file://` URI anchors are renderable by the named downstream consumers; if not, consider making the default an empty string/None (emit no URL when unset) or a relative path without the file:// scheme, with a note in config.py about the intended use case.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a `file://` path to the guide as the default base URL when `REBAR_DOCS_URL` is unset, but does not verify that `file://` URIs with fragment anchors are actually renderable or useful by the downstream consumers named in the plan (code_review/workflow_ops.py coaching[] output, MCP tool responses, CI environments). File URIs are not followed by most CI runners or LLM tool-call contexts, making the default silently inert for the majority of use cases.
findings.location: What section: 'BASE URL: defaults to the REBAR_DOCS_URL env var if set, else a repo-relative file:// path to the guide'; AC4: 'Base URL default (REBAR_DOCS_URL env else relative file://) as a config.py constant'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the zero-argument / empty-string / null criterion-id case to the error-state spec and AC3 proof for both CLI (`rebar explain` with no args → usage + exit non-zero) and MCP (null/missing field → structured error).
findings.finding: The CLI surface `rebar explain` is not specified for the zero-argument invocation (user types `rebar explain` with no criterion-id). No error message, usage hint, or exit code is defined for this entry-point edge case. Similarly, the MCP tool definition does not state what happens when criterion-id is null, empty-string, or missing from the tool call payload.
findings.location: What section — 'rebar explain <criterion-id>' bullet; AC line 'rebar explain BOGUS exits non-zero'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'rebar explain' bullet, add: 'missing-argument (CLI: print usage and exit non-zero; MCP: return structured error with code MISSING_ARGUMENT)'. Extend AC3 to include: `rebar explain` (no args) exits non-zero with a usage hint; MCP call with missing criterion_id returns a structured error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the open 'Decide…' with a stated decision: 'Use auto-generated; record this choice in the guide header (e.g., a `<!-- generated: do not edit -->` comment and a note in docs/README.md).'
findings.finding: The plan says 'Decide auto-generated vs hand-maintained-with-parity (recommend generated) and record it' — the word 'Decide' explicitly defers the design choice to the executor even though a recommendation is given. An executing agent must make and record this choice rather than being handed a decision.
findings.location: What section: 'Decide auto-generated vs hand-maintained-with-parity (recommend generated) and record it'
findings.suggested_fix: Change to: 'Guide is auto-generated from the registry. Record this decision in a `<!-- auto-generated by rebar.llm.plan_review.registry — do not edit by hand -->` comment at the top of the file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC4 into three separate criteria: (1) config.py carries the REBAR_DOCS_URL constant with fallback; (2) pass-4 renderer and CLI text renderer emit `URL#<criterion-id>`; (3) `rebar review-plan --help` output contains the resolved base URL.
findings.finding: AC4 bundles three distinct deliverables — (a) config.py constant, (b) anchor rule implementation in renderers, and (c) review-plan --help epilog update — into a single criterion, making it difficult to judge partial completion and violating the 'coherent single-outcome' measurability check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: fourth bullet '[ ] Base URL default (REBAR_DOCS_URL env else relative file://) as a config.py constant; anchor rule `#<criterion-id>`; review-plan --help epilog carries the base URL'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an expand-contract note or explicit rollback path for the coaching[] URL#anchor change: either emit the anchor as an additional field (leaving existing text unchanged) in a first phase, or document a named back-out step if the format proves incorrect post-merge.
findings.finding: The Pass-4 renderer and `_render_plan_review_text` changes add URL#anchor to the coaching[] output, which is a behavioral change to an interface `code_review/workflow_ops.py` already consumes. The plan acknowledges the consumer and requires a passing test, but specifies no expand-contract sequence (e.g., emit the anchor field additionally before removing the old form) and no rollback/back-out path if the URL change breaks the consumer in production.
findings.location: What section — 'Coach anchored deep-links'; AC: 'Coach output emits stable anchored deep-links; code_review/workflow_ops.py still consumes coaching[] without breakage.'
findings.suggested_fix: Emit the deep-link URL as a separate `coaching_url` field alongside the existing `coaching[]` text in phase 1; update workflow_ops.py to read the new field in phase 2; this gives a safe rollback (drop the new field) without breaking the consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that malformed-registry is caught and isolated to the explain tool (lazy load with try/except returning a structured MCP error), so a corrupt registry file does NOT prevent other MCP tools from serving requests; document this isolation contract in the MCP tool registration code.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the MCP tool returns a structured error payload for the three error states (unknown-id, malformed-registry, missing-file), but does not specify whether a malformed or missing registry causes the explain MCP tool alone to fail or takes down the entire MCP server (and therefore all other tools). In a long-running MCP server, a corrupted registry file at startup or mid-run could silently degrade all unrelated tools if the registry is loaded as a module-level singleton.
findings.location: What / rebar explain; AC line 'MCP tool returns a structured error'
findings.suggested_fix: Load the registry lazily inside explain_criterion (not at module import time) and wrap the load in a try/except that returns a structured MCP error with code 'malformed-registry' on failure. Add a note in the AC: 'A malformed registry must not prevent other registered MCP tools from responding.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the registry shared-state lifecycle: specify load semantics (e.g., loaded once at server start, immutable thereafter), concurrency safety (file read is atomic / no mid-write reads tolerated), and what happens when the file is updated (restart required vs. live reload); capture this in the design doc or a comment in registry.py.
findings.finding: The registry is shared state consumed by three surfaces (CLI, MCP server, passes.py renderer) but the plan does not define its load-time semantics (eager at import vs lazy per-call), ownership (who is authoritative if the file is updated on disk), or behaviour under concurrent MCP requests when the registry file is being updated (TOCTOU race between file-stat and file-read).
findings.location: What / rebar explain; Provenance / 'Symbols introduced by a SIBLING story'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider extracting R-3 (docs/plan-review-gate.md invariant + tuning checklist) into its own leaf ticket, since it is a distinct deliverable for a different persona with no dependency on R-5 tooling.
findings.finding: R-3 (maintainer-facing invariant doc in docs/plan-review-gate.md) is an independently-valuable, independently-releasable deliverable for a distinct persona (maintainers) that has zero dependency on R-5 tooling, yet it is bundled with a large R-5 tooling slice. The plan itself flags them as 'DISTINCT deliverables' and notes they 'may split.' A blocker in MCP tooling or the guide parity work could delay a trivial doc that could have shipped weeks earlier.
findings.location: ## Why / ## What (R-3 bullet)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `explain_criterion` cannot simply be `by_id()[cid]` (already at 20+ sites) at each of the three call sites; if it adds non-trivial logic (e.g., backing-file read + format) that isn't covered by the descriptor, document that logic explicitly to justify a new named function.
findings.finding: `explain_criterion` (proposed as `rebar.llm.plan_review.registry.explain_criterion`) is a premature named abstraction: `registry.by_id()[cid]` already gives the full descriptor at 20+ call sites, and the rubric text is already accessible via the existing `get_prompt()` machinery that `_descriptor_from_prompt` calls. The plan mandates inventing a new named function for three call sites (CLI, MCP, guide parity) that is substantively `by_id()[cid]` + an optional prompt-file read — work the existing API already composes.
findings.location: What section: 'rebar explain <criterion-id>: ONE shared library lookup (name it, e.g. rebar.llm.plan_review.registry.explain_criterion)'
findings.suggested_fix: At each of the 3 call sites, call `registry.by_id()[cid]` directly for the descriptor fields (name, scenario, checklist). Only introduce `explain_criterion` if it bundles non-trivial logic not in the descriptor (e.g., resolving the backing file path for the guide section — which would then also serve the parity check). If so, name and document the extra logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `rebar.llm.plan_review.registry.explain_criterion` is a NEW function to be authored (not a pre-existing symbol to wrap), and add it to the explicit edit-target list for `registry.py`.
findings.finding: The symbol `rebar.llm.plan_review.registry.explain_criterion` named as the shared library lookup does not exist anywhere in the codebase. `registry.py` has no `explain_criterion` function — this is a hallucinated edit target that must be CREATED.
findings.location: What section: 'rebar explain <criterion-id>: ONE shared library lookup (name it, e.g. rebar.llm.plan_review.registry.explain_criterion)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for choosing auto-generated: e.g. 'auto-generated because the guide is fully derivable from the registry's criterion metadata, eliminating the authoring-gap the parity check would need to catch, and ensuring new criteria automatically gain sections without a manual step.'
findings.finding: The plan recommends 'generated' over 'hand-maintained-with-parity' for the authoring guide but states no positive rationale for WHY the generated approach is better. Per G6, the absence of a positive rationale for the chosen approach is a finding.
findings.location: What section — 'Decide auto-generated vs hand-maintained-with-parity (recommend generated) and record it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `explain_criterion(cid: str, *, repo_root=None) -> dict` in `registry.py` (or name the module explicitly) and specify its return schema — at minimum what fields it returns for a valid criterion and what it raises for unknown-id / malformed-registry / missing-file.
findings.finding: The shared library function `rebar.llm.plan_review.registry.explain_criterion` (the plan's named single lookup point for all three surfaces) does not exist anywhere in the codebase. `registry.py` exports `load_criteria`, `by_id`, `check_registry_coverage`, `validate_packaged_routing`, etc. — but no `explain_criterion`. The plan names it as if it already exists.
findings.location: What section — 'rebar explain <criterion-id>' bullet; AC3 ('one shared lookup')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What section: '`explain_criterion(cid, *, repo_root=None) -> dict` returns the merged descriptor from `load_criteria` keyed by `cid` (same shape as `by_id()[cid]`); raises `RegistryError` with a clear message for unknown-id, malformed-registry, and missing backing file.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
